meta.title: The Room That Empties Your Hands
meta.theme: the doorway effect and how the mind files experience by location

format.format_verified: 2026-07: five sentences, TITA, +3 with no negative marking, consistent in released papers 2016 to 2025; re-verify against each new official paper

traps.description: No trap is planted. The outlier shares the field of memory but visibly begins a different discussion, so the item can teach the build first protocol gently.

meta.learning_objective: learn that the odd sentence is found by building the paragraph, not by judging sentences one at a time

source.usage_note: Original composition in CAT register for CAT OS. General cognitive psychology of the doorway effect; no specific text adapted. Personal study only.

meta.prompt_version: in-repo authored (Odd One Out milestone); ODD_MAN_OUT_BIBLE frameworks applied
meta.reviewer_notes: The journey's opening item: a clean expository core about one phenomenon, and an outlier from the same field that plainly begins a different discussion. Deliberately low overlap; a topic matcher succeeds here, and the tiers above take that comfort away. Owner review pending.

sentences.label: A
sentences.text: Psychologists call it the doorway effect: the odd experience of arriving in a room with no idea what you came for.

sentences.label: B
sentences.text: The forgetting, it turns out, is not a glitch but a feature of a mind that files experience scene by scene.

sentences.label: C
sentences.text: During deep sleep, the brain replays the day's events and moves the most important ones into long term storage.

sentences.label: D
sentences.text: In one series of experiments, people who walked through a doorway forgot the objects they were carrying far more often than people who walked the same distance within a single room.

sentences.label: E
sentences.text: Crossing a boundary, researchers suggest, signals that one scene has ended, so the mind archives that scene's details to make room for the next.

builder.prompt: Build the four sentence story of the doorway effect, from naming it to understanding it. The sentence you leave out becomes your answer.

explanation.spine_note: The four sentences form one clean explanation. A names the everyday puzzle, D shows the experiment that pinned it down, E gives the reason the mind does it, and B closes by turning the puzzle into a design feature. Name the effect, show the evidence, explain it, land the point.

core_roles.label: A
core_roles.why_here: It introduces the phenomenon everything else examines, and gives it the name the paragraph keeps using.

core_roles.label: D
core_roles.why_here: It answers the natural question after A, which is how anyone knows this effect is real, with the doorway experiment.

core_roles.label: E
core_roles.why_here: It takes the finding from D and supplies the mechanism, why crossing a boundary should erase anything at all.

core_roles.label: B
core_roles.why_here: It steps back and states what the whole explanation adds up to: the forgetting is the filing system working.

links.explanation: The doorway named in A returns as the experimental doorway in D, the same idea moving from anecdote to evidence.
D's finding becomes E's given: crossing a boundary is now established, and E adds the new material, the reason it wipes the slate.
B's phrase the forgetting points straight back at the loss E just explained, and closes the account of it.

explanation.violation_named: Sentence C is on the topic of memory but structurally alien to this paragraph. The four build one specific account of location triggered forgetting; C opens an entirely different discussion, how sleep strengthens memory, and no sentence here asks or answers that question.
explanation.why_it_breaks: Nothing in the core sets up sleep, and nothing returns to it. C receives no idea from any sentence and passes none on, so there is no join it could occupy. It would sit comfortably in a paragraph about memory consolidation. This paragraph is not that one.
explanation.trap_exposed: The only camouflage here is the shared field. C talks about the brain and memory, so a reader matching topics might hold on to it. But topic is the cheapest thing sentences can share. The four core sentences share something stronger: each one hands its idea to the next, and C never joins that chain.

exclusion_analysis.label: A
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: Judged alone, it reads like a textbook definition floating above the story the others tell.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without A nothing names the effect, so D's experiment and E's mechanism explain a phenomenon the paragraph never introduced.

exclusion_analysis.label: D
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: It is the longest sentence and the only one describing an experiment, so its register feels different from the rest.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without D the claim loses its only evidence, and E explains a finding the paragraph no longer contains.

exclusion_analysis.label: E
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: It is the most abstract of the five, and deciding exactly where it sits takes the most effort.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without E the paragraph jumps from an experimental result straight to a conclusion, with the reason for the forgetting missing entirely.

exclusion_analysis.label: B
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: B and the sleep sentence both make calm general claims about the mind, so a hurried read holds both as candidates and guesses.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without B the explanation ends on mechanism with no meaning drawn, and the paragraph trails off instead of landing.

explanation.detection_habit: Build the four sentence paragraph before judging anything. The odd one is not the sentence that sounds different. It is the sentence the finished paragraph has no seat for.

mentor.challenge: All five sentences live in the world of memory. Only four live in this paragraph. Build first, and the difference will show itself.
mentor.takeaway: Same field is not same paragraph. A sentence belongs to a structure, not to a subject.
